Carlos, you reached the final wording, and now you sign. Let me brief you on the sign-off and on what comes after, because a senior executive thinks past the signature. First, execute the agreement. To execute a contract is to sign it so it becomes legally effective, not just to perform a task. Both sides execute, and that makes the deal real. Second, countersign it. To countersign is to add a second signature that confirms the first, usually from the board or a senior officer. The deal is not sealed until your counterpart countersigns. Third, set the effective date. The effective date is the day the agreement starts to apply, which is not always the day you sign. Read it carefully, because every deadline counts from that date. Fourth, tie up the loose ends. A loose end is a small detail that is still open after the main deal is done, and loose ends become disputes if you ignore them. Make a list and close each one. Fifth, run the handoff. The handoff is the moment you pass the deal to the integration team, with the documents, the timeline, and the open points. A clean handoff protects everything you negotiated. Sixth, plan what comes after with the future perfect. We say: by the close, the teams will have integrated; by the first review, we will have met the targets. The future perfect looks back from a future point at something already finished. Finally, debrief the deal. To debrief is to review, after it is done, what went well and what to do differently next time. So execute, countersign, set the effective date, tie up the loose ends, run the handoff, and debrief. That is how a senior executive seals a deal and looks ahead.

Hi Carlos, it is Diana. The wording is final, the board is ready, and I am calling before the sign-off. First, congratulations. You negotiated this beautifully, and now we seal it. Here is what I need from you on signing day. One, both sides execute the agreement in the morning, and the board will countersign by noon, so confirm the effective date is the first of next month, not the day we sign. Two, before you sign, tie up the two loose ends from the redline, the data clause and the timeline annex, because I do not want them to come back as a dispute. Three, the handoff. Once it is executed, pass the full package to the integration team the same day, with the open points clearly marked. Do not let it sit. Four, look ahead, and be specific. When you brief the board next week, use the future perfect: by the first review, we will have completed the handoff; by the close of the quarter, the teams will have integrated. That tells them exactly where we will be. And five, let us debrief the whole deal together on Friday, the wins and the lessons, while it is fresh. This is your last big step, Carlos. Execute it cleanly, look ahead, and let us seal it. I am proud of this one. Call me when it is signed.

First, tie up the loose ends and disclose every open point. Then both sides execute the agreement and the board countersigns it. Next, confirm the effective date. After that, run the handoff to the integration team and wind down the deal team. Finally, debrief the whole deal, and look ahead to what the teams will have done by the close.
